frame_label: Sindoor, Mangalsutra, Shankha-Pola, Name Change: Looking Married
generated_comment:
A: Kal Sharma ji ki bahu ko dekha, shaadi ke baad sindoor aur mangalsutra mein kitni settled aur grihasth lag rahi thi.
B: Haan sir, aajkal kai ladkiyan office ke naam par yeh sab chhod deti hain.
A: Choice apni jagah hai, lekin hamari parampara ke kuch chinh hote hain; unse hi to suhagan ki pehchaan aur ghar ki maryada dikhti hai.
label: misogynistic
annotation_note: This is misogynistic because it frames a married woman's visible identity markers as necessary for her legitimacy, respectability, and household honor. It encodes the surface manifestations of 'looks more complete/settled after marriage' and disapproval of women who do not wear sindoor or mangalsutra, while masking control as tradition and personal choice.
original_frame: Workplace: Menstrual and Maternity Leave Bias
persona_text: You are Ajay. Age 38. A Rajput man working as a senior government officer in Jaipur. You are sitting in your office with the door closed talking to your junior male colleague after a female officer in your department submitted a formal request for work from home during her periods citing the new government circular. You are commenting on whether such requests should be entertained in a government office setting and what it means for discipline and productivity.
